properties.name: Mission San Diego de Alcalá
properties.summary: First of the chain; destroyed in the 1775 revolt and rebuilt at this valley site.

sources.citation: Founding dates per the standard accounts (Bancroft, Hist. Cal.; Engelhardt).

properties.name: Mission San Carlos Borromeo (Carmel)
properties.summary: Serra's headquarters and burial place; moved from Monterey to Carmel in 1771.

sources.citation: Founding dates per the standard accounts (Bancroft, Hist. Cal.; Engelhardt).

properties.name: Mission San Antonio de Padua
properties.summary: The upper Salinas valley mission, in Salinan country.

sources.citation: Founding dates per the standard accounts (Bancroft, Hist. Cal.; Engelhardt).

properties.name: Mission San Gabriel Arcángel
properties.summary: The richest of the southern missions; Toypurina's 1785 revolt; the gateway of the overland routes.

sources.citation: Founding dates per the standard accounts (Bancroft, Hist. Cal.; Engelhardt).

properties.name: Mission San Luis Obispo de Tolosa
properties.summary: Founded on the strength of the Portolá expedition's bear hunts in the Cañada de los Osos.

sources.citation: Founding dates per the standard accounts (Bancroft, Hist. Cal.; Engelhardt).

properties.name: Mission San Francisco de Asís (Dolores)
properties.summary: The Bay's first mission; its neophyte flights across the water drove the 1795–97 East Bay expeditions.

sources.citation: Founding dates per the standard accounts (Bancroft, Hist. Cal.; Engelhardt).

properties.name: Mission San Juan Capistrano
properties.summary: Founded twice, 1775 and 1776, after the San Diego revolt interrupted the first attempt.

sources.citation: Founding dates per the standard accounts (Bancroft, Hist. Cal.; Engelhardt).

properties.name: Mission Santa Clara de Asís
properties.summary: Paired with the pueblo of San José, whose fields bordered too closely for the friars' comfort.

sources.citation: Founding dates per the standard accounts (Bancroft, Hist. Cal.; Engelhardt).

properties.name: Mission San Buenaventura
properties.summary: The Channel mission Serra founded last in person; armed with cannon in the 1798 census.

sources.citation: Founding dates per the standard accounts (Bancroft, Hist. Cal.; Engelhardt).

properties.name: Mission Santa Bárbara
properties.summary: The 'Queen of the Missions,' beside the presidio; a center of the 1824 Chumash revolt.

sources.citation: Founding dates per the standard accounts (Bancroft, Hist. Cal.; Engelhardt).

properties.name: Mission La Purísima Concepción
properties.summary: Seized by Chumash rebels for a month in 1824; rebuilt after the 1812 earthquake at the present site.

sources.citation: Founding dates per the standard accounts (Bancroft, Hist. Cal.; Engelhardt).

properties.name: Mission Santa Cruz
properties.summary: Across the river from the villa of Branciforte, whose settlers the friars blamed for most of their troubles.

sources.citation: Founding dates per the standard accounts (Bancroft, Hist. Cal.; Engelhardt).

properties.name: Mission Nuestra Señora de la Soledad
properties.summary: The lonely Salinas valley mission; two governors died within its walls.

sources.citation: Founding dates per the standard accounts (Bancroft, Hist. Cal.; Engelhardt).

properties.name: Mission San José
properties.summary: Base of the East Bay and Delta frontier; Estanislao was its alcalde before his 1829 revolt.

sources.citation: Founding dates per the standard accounts (Bancroft, Hist. Cal.; Engelhardt).

properties.name: Mission San Juan Bautista
properties.summary: Sited at 'San Benito' per Lasuén's 1796 reconnaissance synthesis (C-A 50); the 1798 Talholostl battle was fought nearby.

sources.citation: Founding dates per the standard accounts (Bancroft, Hist. Cal.; Engelhardt).

properties.name: Mission San Miguel Arcángel
properties.summary: Sited at 'las Pozas' in the same 1795–96 siting campaign.

sources.citation: Founding dates per the standard accounts (Bancroft, Hist. Cal.; Engelhardt).

properties.name: Mission San Fernando Rey
properties.summary: The valley mission on the LA road; armed with 2-pounders in the 1805 census.

sources.citation: Founding dates per the standard accounts (Bancroft, Hist. Cal.; Engelhardt).

properties.name: Mission San Luis Rey de Francia
properties.summary: Founded 13 Jun 1798, 54 children baptized that day (C-A 24 Doc 277); the largest mission population; Duflot drew its plan in the gallery.

sources.citation: Founding dates per the standard accounts (Bancroft, Hist. Cal.; Engelhardt).

properties.name: Mission Santa Inés
properties.summary: The last Channel mission; the 1824 revolt began here.

sources.citation: Founding dates per the standard accounts (Bancroft, Hist. Cal.; Engelhardt).

properties.name: Mission San Rafael Arcángel
properties.summary: Begun as a hospital asistencia of Dolores, raised to full mission in 1822; the northern frontier's answer to sickness and to Ross.

sources.citation: Founding dates per the standard accounts (Bancroft, Hist. Cal.; Engelhardt).

properties.name: Mission San Francisco Solano (Sonoma)
properties.summary: The last and northernmost mission, founded under Mexican rule partly to face the Russians; seed of Vallejo's Sonoma.

sources.citation: Founding dates per the standard accounts (Bancroft, Hist. Cal.; Engelhardt).

properties.name: Santa Margarita de Cortona (asistencia)
properties.summary: Grain-station and chapel over the cuesta from San Luis Obispo; the rancho kept the name. Sub-station of Mission San Luis Obispo.

sources.citation: Founding dates per the standard accounts (Bancroft, Hist. Cal.; Engelhardt).

properties.name: San Antonio de Pala (asistencia)
properties.summary: The inland asistencia of San Luis Rey; uniquely, still a functioning mission chapel serving a Native community today. Sub-station of Mission San Luis Rey.

sources.citation: Founding dates per the standard accounts (Bancroft, Hist. Cal.; Engelhardt).

properties.name: Las Flores (asistencia)
properties.summary: The coast station between San Luis Rey and San Juan Capistrano; later the Pico brothers' rancho. Sub-station of Mission San Luis Rey.

sources.citation: Founding dates per the standard accounts (Bancroft, Hist. Cal.; Engelhardt).

properties.name: Santa Ysabel (asistencia)
properties.summary: San Diego's mountain asistencia in Kumeyaay country; the same district as the register's Santa Isabel fight of 1826. Sub-station of Mission San Diego.

sources.citation: Founding dates per the standard accounts (Bancroft, Hist. Cal.; Engelhardt).

properties.name: San Bernardino de Sena (estancia)
properties.summary: San Gabriel's estancia at the foot of the pass; the rancho outpost end of the mission system, in the raiding corridor. Sub-station of Mission San Gabriel.

sources.citation: Founding dates per the standard accounts (Bancroft, Hist. Cal.; Engelhardt).

properties.name: Colony Ross
properties.summary: The Russian-American Company's fort and village on Kashaya land, 1812 to 1841. The Presidial System map carries its strength series; Vallejo's 1833 reconnaissance (C-A 53 Doc 45) describes it in full.

sources.citation: Russian establishments per the RAC literature (Gibson; Istomin) and the Spanish-Mexican reconnaissance record (C-A 12, C-A 53).

properties.name: Port Rumiantsev (Bodega)
properties.summary: The company's anchorage and warehouses at Bodega Bay, in use from about 1809. The port the Spanish discovered in 1775, working under a Russian name.

sources.citation: Russian establishments per the RAC literature (Gibson; Istomin) and the Spanish-Mexican reconnaissance record (C-A 12, C-A 53).

properties.name: Kostromitinov Ranch
properties.summary: Company farm on the Russian River plain, one of the three ranches feeding Ross in its last decade. Location approximate.

sources.citation: Russian establishments per the RAC literature (Gibson; Istomin) and the Spanish-Mexican reconnaissance record (C-A 12, C-A 53).

properties.name: Khlebnikov Ranch
properties.summary: Company farm inland from Bodega, named for the RAC agent. Location approximate.

sources.citation: Russian establishments per the RAC literature (Gibson; Istomin) and the Spanish-Mexican reconnaissance record (C-A 12, C-A 53).

properties.name: Chernykh Ranch
properties.summary: The agronomist Chernykh's farm; Voznesensky sketched it in 1841 (the sketch is in this site's gallery). Location approximate.

sources.citation: Russian establishments per the RAC literature (Gibson; Istomin) and the Spanish-Mexican reconnaissance record (C-A 12, C-A 53).

properties.name: Sutter's Fort (New Helvetia)
properties.summary: Sutter's Mexican land-grant colony at the Sacramento-American confluence, founded 1839. Buyer of Ross's movable property in 1841, magnet for the overland emigrants, and the hinge between the mission-rancho world and the American conquest. The anti-Micheltorena revolt charged the governor with his 'relaciones secretas' with Sutter (C-A 61).

sources.citation: Standard accounts (Bancroft, Hist. Cal., IV); cf. Leg. Rec. (C-A 61) Doc 19
